name: Hanukkah
shortDescription: Hanukkah, also known as the Festival of Lights, is an eight-day Jewish holiday commemorating the rededication of the Second Temple in Jerusalem during the Maccabean Revolt.
description: Hanukkah, meaning 'dedication' in Hebrew, is an eight-day Jewish festival that commemorates the rededication of the Second Temple in Jerusalem in the 2nd century BCE. According to the Talmud, after the Seleucid Greek forces were driven out of Jerusalem by the Maccabees, Jewish rebels led by Judah Maccabee, the Temple was found to be desecrated. The Maccabees sought to cleanse and rededicate the Temple, but found only enough consecrated olive oil to keep the menorah (candelabrum) lit for one day. Miraculously, the oil lasted for eight days, which was the time needed to prepare fresh oil. This miracle is the central focus of the Hanukkah celebration. The holiday is observed by lighting the menorah each night for eight nights, adding one candle each night. Other Hanukkah customs include eating foods fried in oil, such as latkes (potato pancakes) and sufganiyot (jelly doughnuts), playing dreidel (a spinning top), and giving Hanukkah gelt (money). Hanukkah is a relatively minor religious holiday but has become a significant cultural celebration, especially in Western societies, often celebrated near Christmas. The themes of religious freedom and perseverance against oppression are central to the holiday's significance.
